coredata.dc:title: Novel in-situ zwitterionization of carbon quantum dots on membrane surface for oil/water separation
coredata.dc:creator: Carballo, Gabriel V.
Hsu, Yu-Xin
Yang, Hong-Li
Lin, Hsin-Yi
Huang, Chin-Ching
Li, Chi-Lan
Leron, Rhoda B.
Tsai, Hui-An
Lee, Kueir-Rarn
coredata.dc:description: Industrial growth led to an increase in the release of hazardous oily wastewater that seriously threatens human health and the ecosystem. Developing polymeric membranes for treating such wastewater has garnered significant attention, yet their fouling resistance remains a challenge. In this study, an anti-fouling zwitterionic cellulose acetate (CA) membrane, called ZQDs-g-CA, was fabricated by grafting carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on the surface of the CA membrane, followed by zwitterionization of CQDs (Zwitterionic carbon quantum dots, ZQDs). The grafting of ZQDs increased the hydrophilicity and oleophobicity of the membrane due to the hydrophilic functional groups present in the nanomaterial. The surface roughness was increased, allowing a greater surface area for contact between water molecules and the membrane. The modified CA membrane demonstrated excellent water flux (602.2 ± 30.2 L m−1 h−1 bar−1 (LMHB)) and oil/water emulsion separation performance, with a permeation flux of 426.1 ± 9.6 LMHB and oil rejection of 99.4 %. Additionally, the anti-fouling tests demonstrated remarkable performance with more than 96 % flux recovery ratio (FRR), 30.35 % reversible fouling ratio (Rr), and 3.05 % irreversible fouling ratio (Rir). The results showed that the in-situ ZQDs on the CA membrane exceeded the 96 % rejection rate for different oil/water emulsions. ZQDs were successfully applied on the CA membrane, which would provide a reference for the application of ZQDs-g-CA in oil/water separation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zwitterionic carbon quantum dots
Tubular membrane
In-situ zwitterionization
Oily emulsion treatment
Anti-fouling

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and surface properties of membranes

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support for this study from the National Science and Technology Council of Taiwan (MOST 111-2221-E-033-005-MY3).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Reaction mechanism of in-situ zwitterionic carbon quantum dots cellulose acetate membrane.
(a) FTIR spectra, (b) XPS survey scan of CA membranes, and C1s narrow scan of (c) CA, (d) CQDs-g-CA, (e) TQDs-g-CA, and (f) ZQDs-g-CA.
Surface EDX images of membranes. (a) CA, (b) CQDs-g-CA, (c) TQDs-g-CA, and (d) ZQDs-g-CA.
SEM and AFM images of CA membranes. (a) CA, (b) CQDs-g-CA, (c) TQDs-g-CA, and (d) ZQDs-g-CA.
The contact angle of CA membranes. (a) water, (b) underwater diesel oil.
Ultrafiltration performance of CA tubular membranes: (a) pure water flux and (b) diesel/water separation performance.
(a) Antifouling performance of CA tubular membranes, (b) antifouling properties after the first cycle, and (c) flux recovery ratio comparison after various cycles.
Hydration capacity of pristine CA, CQDs-g-CA, and ZQDs-g-CA.
Separation efficiency of different types of oily emulsions through ZQDs-g-CA tubular membrane.
Samples of feed (left side) and permeate (right side) of oil/water emulsions. (a) dodecane, (b) diesel, (c) canola oil, and (d) engine oil.
Optical microscope images of feed (a-d)) and permeate (a’-d’) of different oil–water emulsions. (a) dodecane, (b) diesel, (c) canola oil, and (d) engine oil.
Size distribution of different oil droplets in (a) feed and (b) permeate.
Comparison of antifouling performance of hybrid and surface-modified CA membranes.
Pore size, skin-layer thickness, and surface roughness of pristine and modified membranes.
Anti-fouling properties of CA and ZQDs-g-CA tubular membranes after the first cycle and flux recovery ratio (FRR) after 5 cycles.
Comparison of water contact angle (WCA), underwater oil contact angle (UWOCA), pure water flux (J w), and permeation flux (J p) of hybrid and in-situ modified CA membranes.
Comparison of the anti-fouling properties after the first cycle and flux recovery ratio (FRR) of 5 cycles of ZQDs/CA and ZQDs-g-CA tubular membranes.
A comparative analysis of zwitterionic membranes for oil–water separation.

head.ce:title: Novel in-situ zwitterionization of carbon quantum dots on membrane surface for oil/water separation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical, Biological and Materials Engineering and Sciences, Mapúa University, Manila 1002, Philippines

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Biological and Materials Engineering and Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical, Biological and Materials Engineering and Sciences, Mapúa University, Manila 1002, Philippines

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Graduate Studies, Mapúa University, Manila 1002, Philippines
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Graduate Studies, Mapúa University, Manila 1002, Philippines

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: R&D Center for Membrane Technology, Department of Chemical Engineering, Chung Yuan Christian University, Taoyuan 32023, Taiwan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: R&D Center for Membrane Technology, Department of Chemical Engineering, Chung Yuan Christian University, Taoyuan 32023, Taiwan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Industrial growth led to an increase in the release of hazardous oily wastewater that seriously threatens human health and the ecosystem. Developing polymeric membranes for treating such wastewater has garnered significant attention, yet their fouling resistance remains a challenge. In this study, an anti-fouling zwitterionic cellulose acetate (CA) membrane, called ZQDs-g-CA, was fabricated by grafting carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on the surface of the CA membrane, followed by zwitterionization of CQDs (Zwitterionic carbon quantum dots, ZQDs). The grafting of ZQDs increased the hydrophilicity and oleophobicity of the membrane due to the hydrophilic functional groups present in the nanomaterial. The surface roughness was increased, allowing a greater surface area for contact between water molecules and the membrane. The modified CA membrane demonstrated excellent water flux (602.2 ± 30.2 L m−1 h−1 bar−1 (LMHB)) and oil/water emulsion separation performance, with a permeation flux of 426.1 ± 9.6 LMHB and oil rejection of 99.4 %. Additionally, the anti-fouling tests demonstrated remarkable performance with more than 96 % flux recovery ratio (FRR), 30.35 % reversible fouling ratio (Rr), and 3.05 % irreversible fouling ratio (Rir). The results showed that the in-situ ZQDs on the CA membrane exceeded the 96 % rejection rate for different oil/water emulsions. ZQDs were successfully applied on the CA membrane, which would provide a reference for the application of ZQDs-g-CA in oil/water separation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Oil-water separation is a critical process in oil refining, wastewater treatment, and environmental remediation [1]. Conventional approaches for oil–water separation have low efficiency as the tiny oil droplets in water do not undergo phase separation, particularly in the case of emulsified oil with a size of less than 20 μm [2–4]. The utilization of ultrafiltration (UF) membranes presents a viable alternative to conventional methods for oil–water separation owing to their notable selectivity and ease of operation [5].

Polymeric membranes, particularly cellulose acetate (CA), have gained traction in oil–water separation applications owing to their favorable characteristics [6]. CA membranes have hydrophilic properties, facilitating the effective permeation of water molecules [7,8]. Furthermore, it has good separation efficiency due to its unique structure and properties, which facilitate the selective infiltration of water molecules while efficiently excluding oil molecules [9]. Furthermore, in comparison to other membrane materials, the cost of CA is relatively low and well-suited for large-scale applications [10]. However, CA flat-sheet membranes demonstrate restricted mechanical strength, especially in their pristine state, and are susceptible to fouling [6].

The limitation in mechanical strength of CA flat-sheet membranes can be addressed by fabricating them into tubular membranes, commonly referred to as reinforced hollow fiber membranes. Reinforced hollow fiber membranes have several advantages in comparison to flat-sheet membranes, such as favorable mechanical characteristics, a significant surface area, increased porosity, and packing density [11]. A common type of hollow fiber membrane is a braid-reinforced hollow fiber membrane. The mechanical property of such membranes is enhanced by coating a separation layer, such as a polymeric dope, onto a high-strength tubular braid [12]. These membranes are easily scaled up for large-scale fabrication due to their cost-effectiveness and ease of production [13].

Although CA membranes possess intrinsic hydrophilic properties, they are prone to fouling. The occurrence of fouling can be attributed to the accumulation of oil droplets on the surface of the membrane, resulting in membrane instability, reduced flux, and increased energy consumption [14]. The antifouling properties of the CA membrane could be enhanced through modification such as zwitterionization. Zwitterionization refers to the process of adding zwitterionic functional groups to a specific material, such as a polymeric membrane, in order to improve its antifouling and hydrophilic characteristics. Various approaches can be employed to achieve zwitterionization, such as blending zwitterionic polymers, grafting, coating, and surface quaternization [15]. The process of surface quaternization entails the attachment of polymers that possess cationic tertiary amino groups, which then undergo a reaction with anionic moieties to generate zwitterions [16,17]. Guo et al. [18] conducted a study where they developed a functional layer of polyamide by interfacial polymerization. Then, the membrane that had been functionalized underwent a grafting procedure in which 1,3-propanesultone was introduced onto its surface, resulting in the formation of a membrane that had been changed with zwitterionic properties. The membrane demonstrated significant resistance to erythromycin, a high level of permeability, and exceptional resistance to fouling. In another study, Liu et al. [21] employed surface activation techniques and grafted zwitterionic poly(lysinemethacrylamide) into their pristine membrane. When comparing the modified membrane to its pristine membrane, it was observed that the water contact angle decreased from 121.6° to 29.0°. The incorporation of zwitterionic and amide functional groups increased surface roughness and hydration capacity. Hence, several water molecules were drawn and attached to the surface of the membrane via electrostatic and hydrogen bonding interactions. Furthermore, it was shown that the modified membrane exhibited enhanced oil/water emulsion flow and anti-fouling properties. These improvements can be mostly attributed to the membrane's high hydrophilicity.

An emerging nanomaterial specifically carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted considerable interest owing to their wide-ranging applications, such as their utilization for modifying membranes in oil–water separation [19–21]. CQDs provide numerous benefits in comparison to traditional quantum dots, such as advantageous optical properties, minimal toxicity, strong biocompatibility, easy functionalization, significant water solubility, and cost-efficiency [22,23]. The presence of multiple functional groups on the CQDs, such as hydroxyl (–OH) and carboxyl (–COOH) groups, contributes to their hydrophilic characteristics [24]. CQDs can be readily modified with a wide variety of components, allowing for the precise integration of certain functions onto the membrane's surface [25,26]. Since there are multiple functional groups present in the CQDs for easy functionalization, it can be further enhanced through zwitterionization.

Several studies have investigated zwitterionic modification through surface quaternization [16,17,27–29]. However, there remains a research gap regarding the zwitterionic modification of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on CA tubular membranes and its potential application in oil/water separation. In our previous work [30], we integrated zwitterionic carbon quantum dots (ZQDs) nanoparticles into CA tubular membranes, resulting in enhanced hydrophilicity and oleophobicity, consequently improving both permeability and selectivity. Nevertheless, the antifouling characteristics only saw a modest 6 % improvement, particularly in metrics such as flux recovery ratio (FRR), reversible fouling ratio (Rr), and irreversible fouling ratio (Rir). The efficacy of antifouling was reliant on the movement of nanoparticles towards the membrane surface during formation, generating hydration layers. This study aims to enhance the separation performance and antifouling properties of CA tubular membranes for oil/water separation by grafting ZQDs onto the membrane surface to create denser hydration layers. The surface grafting process involves a series of chemical reactions, starting with the synthesis of CQDs reacted with N, N′-diisopropylcarbodiimide (DIC). The membranes were then immersed in the solution to graft the CQDs. The amide group was introduced to the membranes by reacting with excess 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide (EDC). Subsequently, the surface quaternization was accomplished by ring-opening reaction using 1,4-butanesultone (1,4-BS). The CA tubular membranes were produced using the dry-wet spinning technique, and after the grafting of ZQDs, their permeation and antifouling characteristics were determined. This novel in-situ zwitterionic modification on tubular membrane surface demonstrates significant enhancements in hydrophilicity, separation efficiency, and antifouling properties.

Cellulose acetate powder, CA-394-60S, was acquired from Eastman Chemical Co., USA. Polyester (PET) tubular substrates with a diameter of 1.8 mm were procured from King Membrane Energy Technology Inc., Taiwan. N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) and dodecane were obtained from Tedia Co., Inc., USA. Citric acid (anhydrous, 99.5 %), sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) were supplied by Showa Chemical Co. Ltd., Japan. N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide hydrochloride salt (EDC.HCL), 1, 4-butane sultone (1,4-BS), and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were obtained from Alfa Aesar Co. N, N′-diisopropylcarbodiimide (DIC) was procured from Macklin Inc., China. Phosphate buffer solution (PBS) was acquired from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. Analytical grade hydrochloric acid (HCl) was procured from Aencore Chemical Pty., Ltd., Australia. Ethanol (95 %) was procured from Echo Chemical Co. Ltd., Taiwan. Diesel, canola oil, and engine oil were purchased from CPC Corporation (Taiwan), Weiyi Enterprises Company Ltd. (Taiwan), and Exxon Mobil Corporation (USA), respectively.

Figure S1 illustrates the non-solvent-induced phase separation method used to fabricate the braid-reinforced CA tubular membrane. The CA tubular membranes were fabricated using a tubular membrane fabrication machine used in our previous work [30]. The polymer dope solution, consisting of 15 wt% CA, was totally dissolved in NMP at 90 °C and 60 rpm for 8 h. During the tubular membrane fabrication, the polymer dope solution was transferred to a container and then pushed to a spinneret. It was then drawn into the first coagulation bath, where the non-solvent was water, and the temperature was maintained at 25 ± 2 °C. The tubular membranes were fabricated with a take-up speed of 1.0 m/min and a thickness of 225 µm. The take-up wheel of the second coagulation bath was used to retrieve the tubular membranes, which were later soaked in water to eliminate any residual NMP solvent. Lastly, the tubular membranes were stored in deionized (DI) water for characterization and performance tests.

The in-situ zwitterionization of the CA membrane is presented in Fig. 1 . The CQDs were covalently bonded to CA via DIC and NHS coupling. The CQDs and DIC form an unstable intermediate O-acylisourea, which can form desired and undesired by-products (1,3-Diisopropylurea, DIU). NHS was utilized to prevent the formation of undesired by-products. Then, CQDs were grafted on the CA membrane. The surface-modified CQDs were further modified with EDC to attach the tertiary amine functional group. Subsequently, the membrane was modified with 1,4-BS to introduce the sulfonate group.

The CQDs were synthesized using the process with our previous works [31,32]. Ten grams of citric acid were added to a hydrothermal autoclave and heated at 210 °C for 3 h. The formation of orange paste signified the successful synthesis of CQDs. Then, 1 wt% DIC and 0.8 wt% of NHS were added to 1 wt% CQDs aqueous solution and stirred for 4 h at 25 °C. Afterward, the solution was filtered to separate the by-products. The pristine CA tubular membranes were soaked in the solution and ultrasonicated for 24 h. After which, the membranes were washed with DI water. The obtained membranes were identified as CQDs-g-CA. Subsequently, 13.97 g EDC was dissolved in 1.5 L PBS aqueous solution, and the pH was adjusted to 4 by 0.1 M HCl. The CQDs-g-CA membrane was immersed in the prepared solution and ultrasonicated in a dark environment for 4 h at room temperature. The membrane samples were then washed thoroughly using DI water. The acquired membranes were denoted as Tertiary amine quantum dots(TQDs)-g-CA. Lastly, the TQDs-g-CA membrane was immersed in 1 L 50 v/v% ethanol/water solution containing 1.6 g of 1,4-BS and ultrasonicated for 11.0 h under room temperature. The membranes were washed with DI water, and the obtained membranes were designated as ZQDs-g-CA. All membranes were stored in DI water for performance test and characterization.

The chemical and elemental structure of the membranes were analyzed using attenuated total reflectance–Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR, Spectrum One, PerkinElmer, USA) and X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS, VG K-Alpha, ThermoFisher Scientific Inc., United Kingdom). Field emission scanning electron microscopy with energy dispersive X-ray (FESEM-EDX Model S-4800, Hitachi Co., Japan) and atomic force microscopy (AFM, NanoScope V, Bruker Scientific Instruments, USA) were used to examine the morphology of the membranes. ImageJ software was used to measure the pore size and surface porosity of the membrane surface. An automatic interfacial tensiometer (PD-VP, Kyowa Interface Science Co. Ltd., Japan) was used to evaluate the hydrophilicity and oleophobicity of the membranes. Dynamic vapor sorption analyzer (Q5000 SA, TA Instruments, USA) was used to determine the adsorption capacity of the membranes under controlled temperature and humidity.

Figure S2 illustrates a tubular membrane module with an effective length of 15 cm for crossflow filtration. Figure S3 shows the crossflow filtration setup where the permeability and antifouling properties were measured. An oil–water emulsion was prepared by mixing oil and water. 1 g/L SDS was added and stirred at 1,500 rpm for 8 h. The weight ratio of oil and SDS to water was 1:99. The tubular membrane was placed in the filtration setup, and steady-state was completed at 0.25 bar with a flow rate of 1 L min−1 for 30 min. The pure water and permeation flux (J, L m-1h−1 bar−1, LMHB) and oil rejection rate (%R) were calculated using the following equations:
(1)
J
=
m
ρ A t
(2)
% R
=
1 -
C p
C f
× 100 %
where m (g) was the mass collected; ρ (g⋅L-1) was the density of fluid; A (m2) was membrane effective area; t (h) was sampling time; and Cp and Cf were the permeate and feed concentration, respectively, which were measured using a total organic carbon analyzer (Vario TOC Select, Elementar, Germany).

Similar to the UF test, steady state condition was achieved at 0.25 bar with a flow rate of 1 L/min for 30 min. Each cycle of the antifouling test included 60 min of pure water flux (PWF) and 60 min of permeation flux. PWF was measured every 10 min. Then, the feed was changed to oil emulsion. The permeation flux was measured every 10 min. After 1 cycle, the membrane module was removed and the setup was constantly washed with 1000 ppm SDS for 10 min, then with DI water for another 10 min. Afterward, the membrane was placed back in the setup and simple washed with DI water for 10 min. Three and a half cycles of PWF, oil separation, and washing were repeated to test the antifouling properties. The flux recovery ratio (FRR, %), total fouling ratio (Rt, %), reversible fouling ratio (Rr, %), and irreversible fouling ratio (Rir, %) were calculated using the following equations:
(3)
FRR =
J
w , 2
J
w , 1
× 100 %
(4)
R t
=
1 -
J p
J
w , 1
× 100 %
(5)
R r
=
J
w , 2
-
J p
J
w , 1
× 100 %
(6)
R
ir
=
J
w , 1
-
J
w , 2
J
w , 1
× 100 %
where J w,1, J w,2, and J p were initial water flux, water flux after a simple wash, and flux when feed was oil–water emulsion, respectively.

Fig. 2
a displays the data obtained from the ATR-FTIR analysis conducted on the pristine and modified CA membranes. From the pristine CA membrane, the stretching of the hydroxyl group and CH2 was seen at 3500 cm−1 and 2925 cm−1, respectively. The vibrations of ester and hydroxyl groups peaks were exhibited at 1741 cm−1 and 1371 cm−1, respectively [33]. In addition, the observed peaks at 1229 cm−1 and 1042 cm−1 can be ascribed to the presence of the symmetric –ROR linkage found in cellulose esters [34,35]. The pristine and modified CA membranes' spectra were very comparable due to the low concentration of QDs grafted on the membrane, and the functional groups of CA have peaks that coincide with the peaks of QDs. In order to verify the presence of QDs on the membrane surface, additional investigations employing XPS and EDX techniques were carried out.

The elemental composition and XPS survey of the membranes are presented in Table S1 and Fig. 2 b. No detectable nitrogen or sulfur elements were observed in the pure CA membrane. The grafting of CQDs into the surface of the CA membrane altered the carbon-to-oxygen ratio, which can be ascribed to an increase in oxygen atoms originating from the carboxyl groups of CQDs. The nitrogen content of the TQDs-g-CA was observed to be 2.14 % following the grafting process. The observed increase in nitrogen concentration in TQDs-g-CA can be ascribed to the existence of amino groups derived from the tertiary amine within the TQDs. The ZQDs-g-CA demonstrated the presence of nitrogen (1.60 %) and sulfur (0.42 %) elements that originate from the quaternary amine and sulfobetaine groups. The C1s narrow scan of CA membranes is displayed in Fig. 2(c-f). The C – C, C – O, and C=O peaks were located at ∼284, ∼286, and ∼288 eV, respectively. Upon the reaction of EDC with CQDs-g-CA, a new peak was located at 287.4 eV which corresponds to C – N bond from the tertiary amine. After grafting the ZQDs, new peaks were found at 287.4 and 288.5 eV which correspond to C – N+ and C – N, respectively. The new peak of C – N+ indicated the successful grafting of ZQDs. In Figure S4 , the XPS narrow scan of O1s, N1s and S2p of pristine and modified CA membranes are displayed. The O1s narrow scan of the membranes displays the peak corresponding to the C – O and C=O bonds located at ∼ 532, and ∼ 531 eV, respectively. The N1s narrow scan of TQDs-g-CA and ZQDs-g-CA only detected the C – N bond at ∼ 399 eV [36] while the S2p narrow scan detected the SO3 - peak located at 167.6 eV [37].

The elemental distribution of oxygen, carbon, nitrogen, and sulfur on the membrane surface is shown in the EDX images (Fig. 3 ). EDX analysis was used in this study to confirm that QDs were present on the CA membrane's surface. Due to the absence of amino and sulfobetaine groups, both pristine CA and CQDs-g-CA showed no presence of nitrogen and sulfur elements. The nitrogen element, which came from the tertiary amine of TQDs, was found in the TQDs-g-CA EDX images. Furthermore, zwitterionization resulted in the identification of sulfur derived from the sulfobetaine group. The EDX and XPS analyses confirmed the presence of QDs on the surface of the CA membranes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and surface properties of membranes
ce:section.ce:para:
The SEM surface image of the modified CA membrane at 10k magnification is shown in Fig. 4
(a1-d1). It provided evident visual evidence of the changes in the surface resulting from the zwitterionic modification. The observed results can be attributed to the in-situ zwitterionic modification of the CQDs on the membrane surface, forming multiple bonds and layers. Furthermore, the reduction in pore diameter and surface porosity was observed with further modification, as shown in the surface SEM images at 50k magnification (Fig. 4 a2-d2).

Table 1 displays the measurements of pore size, surface porosity, and skin-layer thickness of the membranes. The in-situ zwitterionic modification of CQDs resulted in a decrease in both pore size and surface porosity. However, an inverse relationship was observed between the skin layer thickness and pore size. The skin-layer thickness increased as the membrane was grafted with multiple layers (Fig. 4 a3-d3). The AFM images and root-mean-square (Rq) roughness values are shown in Fig. 4 a4-d4 and Table 1, respectively. All the modified membranes exhibited an increased surface roughness, as observed from the AFM images. The trend was CA < CQDs-g-CA < TQDs-g-CA < ZQDs-g-CA. This result is consistent with the previous study of Cheng et al. [17]. After surface modification, there was a decrease in the surface porosity and pore size and an increase in the surface roughness, implying multiple layers were formed on the membrane surface.

The hydrophilicity of the membrane surface is depicted in Fig. 5
a, as determined through water contact angle measurements. Upon surface modification, the water contact angles of CQDs-g-CA (57.0 ± 1.2°) were decreased compared to the pristine CA (60.5 ± 1.2°). The membrane's enhanced hydrophilicity can be attributed to the grafting of CQDs. Compared to CQDs-g-CA, TQDs-g-CA (55.4 ± 0.7°) contact angle was further decreased due to its further modification on the membrane surface. CQDs possessed carboxylic functional groups, while TQDs contained carboxylic and tertiary amine functional groups. The ZQDs-g-CA exhibited the lowest water contact angle (53.3 ± 1.0°) due to the quaternary amine and sulfonate groups from the ZQDs. In zwitterionization, it is suggested that a hydration layer is generated at the membrane surface due to hydrogen-bonding interactions between water molecules and the amine functional groups. The distinct chemical structure of ZQDs, which contains quaternary amine and sulfonate groups, facilitates a strong binding of water molecules through electrostatic interactions, forming a significantly denser hydration layer on the surface [38]. In addition, it should be noted that the hydrophilicity of the membrane is influenced by the surface roughness of ZQDs-g-CA, as a greater surface area allows for increased contact between water molecules and the membrane.

The underwater oil contact angle is displayed in Fig. 5 b. Diesel droplets were deposited onto the surface of the membrane to determine the underwater oil contact angle. The underwater oil contact angle exhibited an inverse relationship with the water contact angle, which suggests reduced interaction between diesel and the membrane surface. The measured underwater oil contact angle of pristine CA (125.13 ± 1.67°) was found to be lower in comparison to the underwater oil contact angles observed for CQDs-g-CA (133.92 ± 1.45°), TQDs-g-CA (137.93 ± 0.96°), and ZQDs-g-CA (142.02 ± 1.19°). The data suggests that the zwitterionic modification of CQDs onto the CA membrane surface improved the oleophobic characteristics of the membrane.

In this study, the pure water flux and diesel/water emulsion separation performances, including permeate flux and oil rejection rate, were evaluated to evaluate the performance of the modified membranes. As depicted in Fig. 6 , the PWF, permeation flux, and oil rejection for the pristine membrane were measured to be 468.1 ± 38.0 L m-1 h−1 bar−1, 299.7 ± 38.9 L m-1 h−1 bar−1 and 97.9 ± 0.5 %, respectively. After surface modification, the modified membranes exhibited a decrease in pore size and surface porosity (Fig. 4 and Table 1), contributing to an increase in the oil rejection of the membrane. Furthermore, the results showed that the permeability was also increased, which can be attributed to the improved hydrophilicity and oleophobicity of the modified membranes (Fig. 5). Among the modified membranes, ZQDs-g-CA had the highest PWF (602.2 ± 30.2 LMHB) and permeation flux (426.1 ± 9.6 LMHB) with an oil rejection of 99.4 ± 0.5 %. The grafting of ZQDs onto the CA membrane surface introduced various hydrophilic functional groups, including carboxyl, quaternary amine, and sulfonate. This modification resulted in the membrane’s enhanced hydrophilicity and oleophobicity and stronger affinity towards water due to electrostatic interactions, which led to the formation of a hydration layer on the surface.

The antifouling test of the CA and ZQDs-g-CA is shown in Fig. 7
a. There are existing studies that conduct 2 to 3 cycles of testing [30,39–42]. In this study, five cycles of testing were conducted to further observe the performance of pristine and modified membranes. The ZQDs-g-CA membrane showed higher flux than the pristine CA membrane after five cycles of testing. The modified membranes exhibited improved hydrophilicity, which boosted the production of hydration layers on their surfaces and hence decreased their susceptibility to fouling. The electrostatic interaction and hydrogen bonding between the functional groups on a membrane surface modified with zwitterions support the formation of the hydration layer. The observed phenomena can be ascribed to the strong ionic bonds among charged zwitterionic groups, enabling the binding of a significant amount of water molecules and facilitating their mobility. This layer increases the membrane's resistance to fouling agents by creating steric hindrance, which raises the pollutants' Gibbs free energy (G) as they get closer to the membrane's surface [15,18,38].

The observed findings are consistent with the data in Fig. 7 b and Table 2 , which shows the antifouling properties, including flux recovery, reversible fouling, and irreversible fouling ratios. The ZQDs-g-CA exhibited the highest ratio of flux recovery (93.24 %) and reversible fouling (14.13 %). Furthermore, it was observed that the ZQDs-g-CA showed the lowest irreversible fouling ratio, with a measured value of 6.75 %. This enhancement can be attributed to its high hydrophilicity and oleophobicity due to the hydrophilic functional group and formed hydration layers. The FRR data after five cycles is presented in Fig. 6 c and Table 2. The FRR of the ZQDs-g-CA exhibited a consistent value of 81.43 %, which represents a significant enhancement compared to the pristine CA membrane of 62.60 %. The observed improvement can be ascribed to the increased hydrophilicity and antifouling characteristics of the ZQDs-g-CA membrane. The presence of hydration layers on the surface of modified membranes effectively mitigated fouling, which decreased the number of oil molecules adhered to the surface and obstructed the flow of water molecules.

Fig. 8 shows the hydration capacity of the pristine and modified membranes. It showed that the weight percent of modified membranes were higher compared to pristine CA membrane with the ZQDs-g-CA being the highest. It indicates that the ZQDs-g-CA have the highest ability to adsorb water molecules. This shows that upon grafting of ZQDs, it forms a dense hydration layer which contains strong ionic bonds between zwitterionic groups that binds multiple water molecules. This supports the results on hydrophilicity and anti-fouling performance because ZQDs-g-CA have highest hydration capacities and water adsorption properties due to the dense hydration layer formed by grafting of ZQDs making them less susceptible to fouling.

The efficiency of oil–water separation using the ZQDs-g-CA membrane is depicted in Fig. 9 . Various kinds of oil/water emulsions, including dodecane, diesel, canola oil, and engine oil, were employed to assess the efficiency of the membrane in the process of oil/water separation. The oil rejection of ZQDs-g-CA for all oil/water emulsions exceeded 96 %. The findings of this study indicate that ZQDs-g-CA resulted in excellent separation efficiency for various oil/water emulsions.

The sample images of the feed and permeate are depicted in Fig. 10 . The visual representations showed a notable difference between the feed and permeate. The milky white color of the feed was observed, while all the permeatesexhibited a transparent color. Additionally, theoptical microscope images of both the feed and permeate are depicted in Fig. 11 . The visual depictions of the feed demonstrated the presence of significant oil droplets, while the permeates indicated only a small number of oil droplets to an almost clear solution. The results suggest that a considerable quantity of the oil was removed following the crossflow ultrafiltration using ZQDs-g-CA.

Fig. 12 illustrates the size distribution of oil droplets in both the feed and permeate. The highest rejection rate was observed for engine oil emulsion, followed by canola oil, diesel, and dodecane emulsions. These data align with the size distribution of emulsion, as larger oil droplets hinder their ability to penetrate the pores of the membrane effectively. On the contrary, dodecane exhibited the least rejection due to its ability to effectively infiltrate the pores, owing to its small oil droplet size. When examining the size distribution of the permeate and feed solution, it is observed that the permeate curve has a single peak, which is consistent with previous report where the micelles of surfactant were about 50–100 nm, which were formed by the aggregation of SDS in the oil–water system [43].

In our previous work [30], ZQDs nanoparticles were incorporated into the CA tubular membrane. The membrane hydrophilicity and oleophobicity were improved, enhancing the permeability, selectivity, and antifouling properties. In this study, zwitterionic surface modification was done by grafting CQDs and surface quaternization using sultone. The membrane incorporated with ZQDs nanoparticles was denoted as ZQDs/CA.

After the incorporation of ZQDs nanoparticles and surface modification on the CA membrane, the hydrophilicity and oleophobicity were enhanced. Table 3 shows the comparison of water contact angle (WCA), underwater oil contact angle (UWOCA), pure water flux (J w), and permeation flux (J p) of ZQDs/CA and in-situ modified (QDs-g-CA) tubular membranes. The WCA of ZQDs/CA was lower compared to the surface-modified CQDs-g-CA and TQDs-g-CA. The incorporation of ZQDs that contain multiple hydrophilic functional groups, such as carboxyl, amino, and sulfobetaine groups, has resulted in a decrease in the water contact angle. Therefore, the membrane has become more hydrophilic. Furthermore, ZQDs-g-CA was lower compared to ZQDs/CA because the modification was directly made on the surface, which formed a dense hydration layer. Furthermore, the oleophobic properties of all surface-modified membranes were greater than the ZQDs/CA membranes. ZQDs-g-CA has the highest UWOCA among all the modified membranes.

The result of the WCA and UWOCA reflected on the pure water and permeation flux. The pure water flux of ZQDs/CA was greater than the CQDs-g-CA and TQDs-g-CA, while the ZQDs-g-CA was higher than the ZQDs/CA. The permeation flux of all surface-modified membranes was higher than ZQDs/CA, with the ZQDs-g-CA being the highest. These results show that zwitterionic modification enhances the wetting properties of the membrane because of the multiple hydrophilic functional groups, while surface grafting enhances the oleophobic properties of the membrane because the modification was done on the surface, forming a denser hydration layer. The permeation flux of ZQDs/CA and ZQDs-g-CA were higher than those of CQDs-g-CA and TQDs-g-CA because the hydration layer formed contains strong ionic bonds between zwitterionic groups that can bind to multiple water molecules and can also allow the mobility of these water molecules.

Fig. 13 compares the antifouling performance of the ZQDs/CA and ZQDs-g-CA. The ZQDs-g-CA exhibited the highest flux after five and a half cycles of testing. The findings are consistent with the data shown in Table 4 for the anti-fouling properties: flux recovery ratio (FRR), reversible fouling ratio (Rr), and irreversible fouling ratio (Rir). Comparing the hybrid and in-situ modification, the FRR of ZQDs-g-CA increased from 88.42 % to 93.24 %. Furthermore, the Rt and Rir of ZQDs-g-CA decreased from 39.69 to 20.89 % and 11.57 to 6.75 %, respectively. After three cycles, the FRR data of ZQDs-g-CA was consistently maintained at 88.00 %, representing a significant enhancement compared to the FRR of ZQDs/CA of 77.83 %. Furthermore, the FRR after 5 cycles was higher than the third cycle with a value of 81.43 %. The mechanism of zwitterionization mainly focuses on forming a hydration layer on the membrane surface, introducing steric hindrance that increases the Gibbs free energy of pollutants as they come in contact with the membrane surface [26]. The process of forming the hydration layer for incorporating ZQDs (ZQDs/CA) depends on the migration of nanoparticles to the membrane surface during membrane formation. The antifouling performance and properties of ZQDs-g-CA were higher compared to the ZQDs/CA because the modification was directly made on the membrane surface. Therefore, denser hydration layers were made through the surface modification of the membrane compared to the incorporation of ZQDs. In conclusion, novel in-situ zwitterionization of CQDs boosted the hydrophilicity and oleophobicity, which further enhanced the anti-fouling performance of the CA tubular membrane.

Table 5 presents a comparative analysis between the current study and other zwitterionic-modified membranes employed in the process of oil–water separation. It can be observed that the ZQDs-g-CA exhibited a considerable PWF, permeation flux, and selectivity compared to the existing zwitterionic-modified membrane. The findings indicate that the ZQDs-g-CA tubular membrane was efficient for the separation of oil and water, demonstrating its potential suitability for commercial applications.

A novel in-situ zwitterionic CA membrane was fabricated by grafting CQDs. The carboxyl groups of CQDs could be modified by using excess EDC, whose tertiary amine group could be used to create zwitterionic moieties via surface quaternization using 1,4-butanesultone. The zwitterionic modification increased the surface roughness and enhanced the hydrophilicity and oleophobicity of the membranes. The ZQDs-g-CA membrane demonstrated an excellent ultrafiltration performance in terms of pure water flux (602.2 ± 30.2 LMHB), permeation flux (426.1 ± 9.6 LMHB), and separation efficiency (99.4 %). Furthermore, the ZQDs-g-CA exhibited favorable antifouling properties, (FRR = 96.94 %, Rr = 30.35, Rir = 3.05 %). In addition, ZQDs-g-CA exhibited high oil rejection rates (96.6 % to 99.5 %) when subjected to various oil–water emulsions such as dodecane, diesel, canola oil, and engine oil. In conclusion, the ZQDs-g-CA tubular membrane exhibited excellent performance and separation efficiency and can be potentially used for efficient oil/water separation.

Gabriel V. Carballo: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Conceptualization. Yu-Xin Hsu: Visualization, Methodology, Conceptualization. Hong-Li Yang: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Hsin-Yi Lin: Validation. Chih-Ching-Huang: . Chi-Lan Li: Visualization. Rhoda B. Leron: Writing – original draft, Validation. Hui-An Tsai: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization. Kueir-Rarn Lee: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization.

ce:section.ce:para:
